title: National Health Service

infobox.value: (**NHS**) is the collective term for the four separate [publicly funded healthcare systems](/wiki/Publicly_funded_health_care "Publicly funded health care") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"): the [National Health Service (England)](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)"), [NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland"), [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales"), and [Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland) "Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)") which was created separately and is often referred to locally as "the NHS".[[2]](#cite_note-3) The original systems were established in 1948 (NHS [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")/GIG [Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru") was founded in 1969) as part of major social reforms following the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), and officially launched at Park Hospital in [Davyhulme](/wiki/Davyhulme "Davyhulme"), near Manchester, England (now known as Trafford General Hospital). The founding principles were that services should be comprehensive, universal and free at the point of delivery.[[3]](#cite_note-4) Each system provides a comprehensive range of [health services](/wiki/Health_services "Health services"), provided without charge for residents of the United Kingdom apart from dental treatment and optical care,[[4]](#cite_note-entitle-5) though NHS patients in [England](/wiki/England "England") who are not exempt have to pay [prescription charges](/wiki/Prescription_charges "Prescription charges").[[5]](#cite_note-exemptper-6)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Publicly-funded healthcare systems in the United Kingdom

"NHS" redirects here. For other uses, see [NHS (disambiguation)](/wiki/NHS_(disambiguation) "NHS (disambiguation)").

For the individual national healthcare services, see [National Health Service (England)](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)"), [NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland"), [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales"), and [Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland) "Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)").

Logos of the National Health Service

[![Logo of the NHS used in England](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/National_Health_Service_%28England%29_logo.svg/250px-National_Health_Service_%28England%29_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:National_Health_Service_(England)_logo.svg)

Logo of [NHS](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)") in England[[a]](#cite_note-1)[[1]](#cite_note-2)

[![Logo of the health service in Northern Ireland](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/58/Health_and_Social_Care_%28Northern_Ireland%29_logo.svg/250px-Health_and_Social_Care_%28Northern_Ireland%29_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland)_logo.svg)

Logo of [HSC](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland) "Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)") in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")

[![Logo of the NHS in Scotland](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/0e/NHS_Scotland_logo.svg/250px-NHS_Scotland_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NHS_Scotland_logo.svg)

Logo of [NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland")

[![Logo of the NHS in Wales](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e9/NHS_Wales_logo.svg/250px-NHS_Wales_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NHS_Wales_logo.svg)

Logo of [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales")

The **National Health Service** (**NHS**) is the collective term for the four separate [publicly funded healthcare systems](/wiki/Publicly_funded_health_care "Publicly funded health care") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"): the [National Health Service (England)](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)"), [NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland"), [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales"), and [Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland) "Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)") which was created separately and is often referred to locally as "the NHS".[[2]](#cite_note-3) The original systems were established in 1948 (NHS [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")/GIG [Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru") was founded in 1969) as part of major social reforms following the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), and officially launched at Park Hospital in [Davyhulme](/wiki/Davyhulme "Davyhulme"), near Manchester, England (now known as Trafford General Hospital). The founding principles were that services should be comprehensive, universal and free at the point of delivery.[[3]](#cite_note-4) Each system provides a comprehensive range of [health services](/wiki/Health_services "Health services"), provided without charge for residents of the United Kingdom apart from dental treatment and optical care,[[4]](#cite_note-entitle-5) though NHS patients in [England](/wiki/England "England") who are not exempt have to pay [prescription charges](/wiki/Prescription_charges "Prescription charges").[[5]](#cite_note-exemptper-6)

Taken together, the four systems in 2015–16 employed around 1.6 million people with a combined budget of £136.7 billion.[[6]](#cite_note-7) In 2024, the total health sector workforce across the United Kingdom was 1,499,368.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

When purchasing consumables such as medications, the four healthcare systems have significant market power that influences the global price, typically keeping prices lower.[[11]](#cite_note-12) A small number of products are procured jointly through contracts shared between systems.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Several other countries directly rely on Britain's assessments for their own decisions on state-financed drug reimbursements.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [History of the National Health Service](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Health_Service "History of the National Health Service"), [History of the National Health Service (England)](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Health_Service_(England) "History of the National Health Service (England)"), [History of NHS Scotland](/wiki/History_of_NHS_Scotland "History of NHS Scotland"), and [History of NHS Wales](/wiki/History_of_NHS_Wales "History of NHS Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/BevanMP.jpg/250px-BevanMP.jpg)](/wiki/File:BevanMP.jpg)

[Aneurin Bevan](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan "Aneurin Bevan"), the founder of the NHS[[14]](#cite_note-Thomas-Symonds-15)

Calls for a "unified medical service" can be dated back to the [Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_report_(Poor_Law) "Minority report (Poor Law)") of the [Royal Commission on the Poor Law](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_the_Poor_Laws_and_Relief_of_Distress_1905%E2%80%9309 "Royal Commission on the Poor Laws and Relief of Distress 1905–09") in 1909.[[15]](#cite_note-16) However, following the [Dewar Report](/wiki/Dewar_Report "Dewar Report") of 1912, the [Highlands and Islands Medical Service](/wiki/Highlands_and_Islands_Medical_Service "Highlands and Islands Medical Service") was brought into being that provided centrally planned healthcare services, over half of Scotland's landmass, at minimal cost to patients – in many ways a prototype for NHS Scotland.

[Somerville Hastings](/wiki/Somerville_Hastings "Somerville Hastings"), President of the [Socialist Medical Association](/wiki/Socialist_Medical_Association "Socialist Medical Association"), successfully proposed a resolution at the 1934 [Labour Party Conference](/wiki/Labour_Party_Conference "Labour Party Conference") that the party should be committed to the establishment of a State Health Service.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Following the 1942 [Beveridge Report](/wiki/Beveridge_Report "Beveridge Report")'s recommendation to create "comprehensive health and rehabilitation services for prevention and cure of disease", cross-party consensus emerged on introducing a National Health Service of some description.[[17]](#cite_note-Beveridge_Report-18) Conservative MP and Health Minister, [Henry Willink](/wiki/Henry_Willink "Henry Willink") later advanced this notion of a National Health Service in 1944 with his consultative White Paper "A National Health Service" which was circulated in full and short versions to colleagues, as well as in newsreel.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

When [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee")'s [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") won the [1945 election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") he appointed [Aneurin Bevan](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan "Aneurin Bevan") as [Health Minister](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Health_and_Social_Care "Secretary of State for Health and Social Care"). Bevan then embarked upon what the official historian of the NHS, [Charles Webster](/wiki/Charles_Webster_(historian_of_medicine) "Charles Webster (historian of medicine)"), called an "audacious campaign" to take charge of the form the NHS finally took.[[19]](#cite_note-20) Bevan's National Health Service was proposed in Westminster legislation for [England and Wales](/wiki/National_Health_Service_Act_1946 "National Health Service Act 1946") from 1946 and [Scotland](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(Scotland)_Act_1947 "National Health Service (Scotland) Act 1947") from 1947, and the [Northern Ireland Parliament](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Parliament "Northern Ireland Parliament")'s Public Health Services Act 1947.[[20]](#cite_note-21)
[NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales") was split from [NHS (England)](/wiki/NHS_(England) "NHS (England)") in 1969 when control was passed to the [Secretary of State for Wales](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Wales "Secretary of State for Wales").[[21]](#cite_note-22) According to one history of the NHS, "In some respects the war had made things easier. In anticipation of massive air raid casualties, the Emergency Medical Service had brought the country's municipal and voluntary hospitals into one umbrella organisation, showing that a national hospital service was possible".[[22]](#cite_note-23) Webster wrote in 2002 that "the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") achieved in months what had defeated politicians and planners for at least two decades".[[23]](#cite_note-24)

The NHS was born out of the ideal that healthcare should be available to all, regardless of wealth. Although being freely accessible regardless of wealth maintained [Henry Willink](/wiki/Henry_Willink "Henry Willink")'s principle of free healthcare for all, Conservative MPs were in favour of maintaining local administration of the NHS through existing arrangements with local authorities fearing that an NHS which owned hospitals on a national scale would lose the personal relationship between doctor and patient.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Conservative MPs voted in favour of their amendment to Bevan's Bill to maintain local control and ownership of hospitals and against Bevan's plan for national ownership of all hospitals. The Labour government defeated Conservative amendments resulting in the implementation of a centralised health service model.

The National Health Service has never been a single organisation. The initial structure in 1948 introduced a number of management bodies for England and Wales. These were as follows: [[25]](#cite_note-26)

* 14 regional hospital boards (RHBs)
* 36 boards of governors for teaching hospitals (BGs)
* 388 hospital management committees (HMCs)
* 138 executive councils (ECs)
* 147 local health authorities (LHAs)

Local voluntary hospitals were brought into national public ownership.[[26]](#cite_note-auto-27) On the 5 July 1948, 1,143 voluntary hospitals comprising approximately 90,000 beds and 1,545 municipal hospitals with around 390,000 beds were taken over by the NHS in England and Wales.[[26]](#cite_note-auto-27)

Community and domiciliary health services such as home nurses, public and environmental health and health prevention/promotion continued to be run by separate, elected local municipal Authorities or Councils.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The first [general medical services](/wiki/General_medical_services "General medical services") contract was brought in as part of the NHS's inception. This built on arrangements with [general practitioners](/wiki/General_practitioners "General practitioners") (GP's) as part of the [National Insurance](/wiki/National_Insurance "National Insurance") Act 1911. This resulted initially in 18,000 self-employed general practitioners.[[26]](#cite_note-auto-27)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

At its launch by Bevan on 5 July 1948 it had at its heart three core principles: That it meet the needs of everyone, that it be free at the point of delivery, and that it be based on clinical need, not ability to pay.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

Three years after the founding of the NHS, Bevan resigned from the [Labour government](/wiki/Attlee_ministry "Attlee ministry") in opposition to the introduction of charges for the provision of dentures, dentists,[[31]](#cite_note-32) and [glasses](/wiki/Glasses "Glasses"); resigning in support was fellow minister and future [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister") [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson").[[32]](#cite_note-33) The following year, [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s [Conservative government](/wiki/Third_Churchill_ministry "Third Churchill ministry") introduced prescription fees. However, Wilson's government abolished them in 1965; they were later re-introduced but with exemptions for those on low income.[[33]](#cite_note-34) These charges were the first of many controversies over changes to the NHS throughout its history.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") Labour governments would later implement large scale financing arrangements with private builders in [private finance initiatives](/wiki/Private_finance_initiative "Private finance initiative") and joint ventures.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

From its earliest days, the [cultural history of the NHS](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Health_Service#Cultural_History_of_the_NHS "History of the National Health Service") has shown its place in British society reflected and debated in film, TV, cartoons and literature. The NHS had a prominent slot during the [2012 London Summer Olympics opening ceremony](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony#Second_to_the_right,_and_straight_on_till_morning_(21:35–21:47) "2012 Summer Olympics opening ceremony") directed by [Danny Boyle](/wiki/Danny_Boyle "Danny Boyle"), being described as "the institution which more than any other unites our nation".[[36]](#cite_note-37)

## Eligibility for treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Eligibility for treatment")]

See also: [Immigration health surcharge](/wiki/Immigration_health_surcharge "Immigration health surcharge")

Some health services are free to everyone, including [accident and emergency](/wiki/Emergency_department "Emergency department") room treatment, registering with a GP and attending GP appointments, treatment for some [infectious diseases](/wiki/Infection "Infection"), [compulsory psychiatric treatment](/wiki/Involuntary_commitment "Involuntary commitment"), and some [family planning services](/wiki/Family_planning "Family planning").[[37]](#cite_note-:3-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

Everyone [living in the UK](/wiki/Ordinarily_resident_status "Ordinarily resident status") can use the NHS without being asked to pay the full cost of the service, though [NHS dentistry](/wiki/NHS_dentistry "NHS dentistry") and optometry have standard charges in each of the four national health services in the UK.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Most patients in England have to pay charges for prescriptions though some patients are exempted.[[5]](#cite_note-exemptper-6)

People who are not [ordinarily resident](/wiki/Ordinarily_resident_status "Ordinarily resident status") (including [British citizens](/wiki/British_citizens "British citizens") who may have paid [National Insurance](/wiki/National_Insurance "National Insurance") contributions in the past) may have to pay for services, with some exceptions such as [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee").[[4]](#cite_note-entitle-5)[[40]](#cite_note-41) Patients who do not qualify for free treatment are asked to pay in advance or to sign a written promise to pay for treatment.[[37]](#cite_note-:3-38)

There are some other categories of people who are exempt from the residence requirements such as specific government workers, those in the [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces") stationed overseas, and those working outside the UK as a [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") for an organisation with its principal place of business in the UK.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Citizens of the EU or [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") (EEA) nations holding a valid [European Health Insurance Card (EHIC)](/wiki/European_Health_Insurance_Card "European Health Insurance Card") and people from certain other countries with which the UK has reciprocal arrangements concerning health care can get NHS emergency treatment without charge.[[37]](#cite_note-:3-38)[[42]](#cite_note-43) People from the EU without an EHIC, Provisional Replacement Certificate (PRC)[[43]](#cite_note-44) or S1 or S2 visa may have to pay.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

People applying for a [visa](/wiki/Visa_policy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Visa policy of the United Kingdom") or immigration application for more than six months have to pay an [immigration health surcharge](/wiki/Immigration_health_surcharge "Immigration health surcharge") when applying for their visa and can then get treatment on the same basis as a resident. There are some people who do not have to pay, including health and care workers and their dependents, dependents of some members of the armed forces, and victims of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") or [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence").[[45]](#cite_note-46) In 2024, the charges were £776 per year for students, their dependents, those on Youth Mobility Schemes, and those under aged 18, and £1,035 for all other applicants who are not covered by exemptions.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

## Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Funding")]

The NHS is funded from the general budget, partially accounted for by [National Insurance](/wiki/National_Insurance "National Insurance") contributions, plus around 1% of patient charges for some services.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-48)

In 2022/3, £181.7 billion was spent by the Department of Health and Social Care on services in England. More than 94% of spend was on salaries and medicines.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-48)

In 2024/5, the NHS in Wales budgeted £11.74 billion for health and social care, which was 49% of its budget.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

£19.5 billion was budgeted for health and social care in Scotland for 2024/5.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

£7.3 billion was budgeted for health in Northern Ireland in 2024/5.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

## Staffing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Staffing")]

England's NHS is the largest employer in Europe, with one in every 25 adults in England working for the NHS.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Nursing staff accounted for the largest cohort at more than 330,000 employees, followed by clinical support staff at 290,000, scientific and technical staff at 163,000 and physicians at 133,000.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

## Research and trials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Research and trials")]

In January 2026, NHS England launched a trial combining artificial intelligence (AI) and robot-assisted care to improve the speed and accuracy of lung cancer detection and diagnosis. The initiative coincided with NHS England’s commitment to offer lung cancer screening to all current and former smokers by 2030. NHS England stated that the planned screening expansion could result in an estimated 50,000 lung cancer diagnoses by 2035, including around 23,000 identified at an early stage, potentially saving thousands of lives.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

## Issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Issues")]

### Funding and costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Funding and costs")]

The funding of the NHS is usually an election issue, and fell under scrutiny during the Covid-19 pandemic.

In July 2022, *The Telegraph* reported that think tank [Civitas](/wiki/Civitas_(think_tank) "Civitas (think tank)") found that health spending was costing about £10,000 per household in the UK. They said that this was the third highest share of GDP of any nation in Europe and claimed that the UK "has one of the most costly health systems – and some of the worst outcomes". The findings were made before the government increased health spending significantly, with a 1.25% increase in [National Insurance](/wiki/National_Insurance "National Insurance"), in April 2022. Civitas said that "runaway" health spending in the UK had increased by more than any country despite the drop in national income due to the COVID pandemic.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The Labour Government elected in 2024 stated that their policy was that the "NHS is broken".[[55]](#cite_note-56) They announced an immediate stocktake of current pressures led by Labour peer [Lord Ara Darzi](/wiki/Ara_Darzi,_Baron_Darzi_of_Denham "Ara Darzi, Baron Darzi of Denham").[[56]](#cite_note-57) This was to be followed by development of a new "10 year plan" for the NHS to replace the [NHS Long Term Plan](/wiki/NHS_Long_Term_Plan "NHS Long Term Plan") published in 2019.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

The potential rise of the cost of [social care](/wiki/Social_care_in_the_United_Kingdom "Social care in the United Kingdom") has been signalled by research. Professor [Helen Stokes-Lampard](/wiki/Helen_Stokes-Lampard "Helen Stokes-Lampard") of the [Royal College of GPs](/wiki/Royal_College_of_GPs "Royal College of GPs") said in 2017, "It's a great testament to medical research, and the NHS, that we are living longer – but we need to ensure that our patients are living longer with a good quality of life. For this to happen we need a properly funded, properly staffed health and social care sector with general practice, hospitals and social care all working together – and all communicating well with each other, in the best interests of delivering safe care to all our patients".[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Employment and waiting lists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Employment and waiting lists")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/EU_workers_joining_and_leaving_the_NHS%2C_annual_variation_in_absolute_numbers_%282012-2017%29.svg/500px-EU_workers_joining_and_leaving_the_NHS%2C_annual_variation_in_absolute_numbers_%282012-2017%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_workers_joining_and_leaving_the_NHS,_annual_variation_in_absolute_numbers_(2012-2017).svg)

EU workers joining and leaving the NHS in England, annual variation in absolute numbers (2012–2017)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

Joiner

Leaver

In June 2018, the [Royal College of Physicians](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Physicians "Royal College of Physicians") calculated that medical training places need to be increased from 7,500 to 15,000 by 2030 to take account of part-time working among other factors. At that time there were 47,800 consultants working in the UK of which 15,700 were physicians. About 20% of consultants work less than full-time.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

On 6 June 2022, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* said that a survey of more than 20,000 frontline staff by the nursing trade union and professional body, the [Royal College of Nursing](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Nursing "Royal College of Nursing"), found that only a quarter of shifts had the planned number of registered nurses on duty.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

The NHS will potentially have a shortage of [general practitioners](/wiki/General_practitioner "General practitioner"). From 2015 to 2022, the number of GPs fell by 1,622 and some of those continuing to work have changed to work part-time.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

In June 2023, the delayed NHS Long Term Workforce Plan was announced, to train doctors and nurses and create new roles within the health service.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

The Welsh and UK governments announced a partnership on 23 September 2024 to reduce NHS waiting lists in England and Wales during the Labour Party Conference in Liverpool. This collaboration aimed to share best practices and tackle common challenges. Previously, [Eluned Morgan](/wiki/Eluned_Morgan "Eluned Morgan") rejected a Conservative proposal for treating Welsh patients in England. The Welsh Conservatives welcomed the new partnership as overdue, while [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") criticised it as insufficient for addressing deeper issues within the Welsh NHS.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

#### Mental health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mental health")]

Further information: [Mental health in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Mental_health_in_the_United_Kingdom "Mental health in the United Kingdom")

One in four patients throughout the UK wait more than three months to see an NHS mental health professional, with 6% waiting at least a year.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

The [National Audit Office](/wiki/National_Audit_Office_(United_Kingdom) "National Audit Office (United Kingdom)") found mental health provisions for children and young people will not meet growing demand, despite promises of increased funding. Currently one-quarter of young people needing mental health services can get NHS help. The [Department of Health and Social Care](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Social_Care "Department of Health and Social Care") hopes to raise the ratio to 35%. Efforts to improve mental health provisions could reveal previously unmet demand.[[66]](#cite_note-theguardian.com-67)

In March 2025 The labour government announced it would be making some changes to the [Personal Independence Payment](/wiki/Personal_Independence_Payment "Personal Independence Payment") (PIP) eligibility for some mental health conditions, taking well needed funds from patients [[67]](#cite_note-bigissue.com-68) to support its NHS resident doctors 29% wage increase after industrial action provoked by the [British Medical Association](/wiki/British_Medical_Association "British Medical Association")
[[68]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-69)

### Inclusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Inclusion")]

In 2024, some NHS hospitals required [radiographers](/wiki/Radiographer "Radiographer") to ask all male patients aged 12 to 55 if they were pregnant for inclusivity purposes. This received significant coverage in the media.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

### Workplace inclusivity and anti-racism measures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Workplace inclusivity and anti-racism measures")]

UK Health Secretary [Wes Streeting](/wiki/Wes_Streeting "Wes Streeting") warned in 2025 of a “rising tide of racism” reminiscent of the 1970s and 1980s, noting that such attitudes were becoming “permissible again” and were affecting NHS staff and patients.[[73]](#cite_note-GuardianRacism-74) According to the [Royal College of Nursing](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Nursing "Royal College of Nursing") (RCN), reports from nurses of racist incidents at work rose by 55% between 2022 and 2025. The RCN expects to receive more than 1,000 calls in 2025 from nurses seeking advice and support after experiencing racism, compared with almost 700 in 2022.[[74]](#cite_note-GuardianNurses-75)[[75]](#cite_note-HCMAbuse-76)

Alongside concerns about racism, the government commissioned an independent review into antisemitism within the NHS after reports that “clear cases were not dealt with adequately”.[[76]](#cite_note-GuardianReview-77) A report by the [General Medical Council](/wiki/General_Medical_Council "General Medical Council") (GMC) indicated that Jewish doctors have experienced increased antisemitic abuse from colleagues since [7 October 2023](/wiki/October_7_attacks "October 7 attacks"), prompting calls for stronger protections and disciplinary processes.[[77]](#cite_note-HCMAntisemitism-78)

NHS England subsequently issued a letter to all NHS trusts, signed by chief executive Sir [Jim Mackey](/wiki/Jim_Mackey_(healthcare_administrator) "Jim Mackey (healthcare administrator)") and chief workforce officer Jo Lenaghan, calling for renewed efforts to ensure workplaces where “staff and patients alike feel safe and welcome” and reaffirming a zero-tolerance stance on hatred, antisemitism, racism, and all discriminatory behaviour.[[78]](#cite_note-TelegraphLaw-79)

## Performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Performance")]

See also: [Health system § Health systems performance](/wiki/Health_system#Health_systems_performance "Health system")

Performance of the NHS is generally assessed separately at the level of England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland.

Since 2004 the left-leaning [Commonwealth Fund](/wiki/Commonwealth_Fund "Commonwealth Fund"), a US health charity, has produced surveys, "Mirror, Mirror on the Wall", comparing the performance of health systems in 11 wealthy countries in which the UK generally ranks highly. For example in 2010 the report put the United Kingdom health systems above those of Germany, Canada and the United States; the NHS was deemed the most efficient among those health systems studied.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81) In the 2021 survey the NHS dropped from first overall to fourth as it had fallen in key areas, including 'access to care and equity'.[[81]](#cite_note-82)

The [Euro Health Consumer Index](/wiki/Euro_Health_Consumer_Index "Euro Health Consumer Index") attempted to rank the NHS against other European health systems from 2014 to 2018, ranking it 16th out of 35 countries in 2018.

The right-leaning[[82]](#cite_note-83) think tank [Civitas](/wiki/Civitas_(think_tank) "Civitas (think tank)") produced an International Health Care Outcomes Index in 2022 ranking the performance of the UK health care system against 18 similar, wealthy countries since 2000. It excluded the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic as data stopped in 2019. The UK was near the bottom of most tables except households who faced catastrophic health spending.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

A 2018 study by the [King's Fund](/wiki/King%27s_Fund "King's Fund"), [Health Foundation](/wiki/Health_Foundation "Health Foundation"), [Nuffield Trust](/wiki/Nuffield_Trust "Nuffield Trust"), and the [Institute for Fiscal Studies](/wiki/Institute_for_Fiscal_Studies "Institute for Fiscal Studies") to mark the NHS 70th anniversary concluded that the main weakness of the NHS was healthcare outcomes. Mortality for cancer, heart attacks and stroke, was higher than average among comparable countries. The NHS was doing well at protecting people from heavy financial costs when ill. Waiting times were about the same, and the management of longterm illness was better than in other comparable countries. Efficiency was good, with low administrative costs and high use of cheaper generic medicines.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Twenty-nine hospital trusts and boards out of 157 had not met any waiting-time target in the year 2017–2018.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

In 2019, *The Times*, commenting on a study in the *British Medical Journal*, reported that "Britain spent the least on health, £3,000 per person, compared with an average of £4,400, and had the highest number of deaths that might have been prevented with prompt treatment". The *BMJ* study compared "the healthcare systems of other developed countries in spending, staff numbers and avoidable deaths".[[86]](#cite_note-87)

More than 130,000 deaths since 2012 in the UK could have been prevented if progress in public health policy had not stopped due to [austerity](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme#Effects "United Kingdom government austerity programme"), analysis by the [Institute for Public Policy Research](/wiki/Institute_for_Public_Policy_Research "Institute for Public Policy Research") found. Dean Hochlaf of the IPPR said: "We have seen progress in reducing preventable disease flatline since 2012".[[87]](#cite_note-88)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Overall satisfaction with the NHS in 2021 fell, more sharply in Scotland than in England, 17 points to 36% – the lowest level since 1997 according to the [British Social Attitudes Survey](/wiki/British_Social_Attitudes_Survey "British Social Attitudes Survey"). Dissatisfaction with hospital and GP waiting times were the biggest cause of the fall.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

The [NHS Confederation](/wiki/NHS_Confederation "NHS Confederation") polled 182 health leaders and 9 in 10 warned that inadequate capital funding harmed their "ability to meet safety requirements for patients" in health settings including hospitals, ambulance, community and mental health services and GP practices.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### Public perception of the NHS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Public perception of the NHS")]

In 2016 it was reported that there appeared to be support for higher taxation to pay for extra spending on the NHS as an opinion poll in 2016 showed that 70% of people were willing to pay an extra penny in the pound in income tax if the money were ringfenced and guaranteed for the NHS.[[90]](#cite_note-91) Two thirds of respondents to a [King's Fund](/wiki/King%27s_Fund "King's Fund") poll, reported in September 2017, favoured increased taxation to help finance the NHS.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

A [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") poll reported in May 2018 showed that 74% of the UK public believed there were too few nurses.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

The trade union, [Unite](/wiki/Unite_the_Union "Unite the Union"), said in March 2019 that the NHS had been under pressure as a result of economic austerity.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

A 2018 public survey reported that public satisfaction with the NHS had fallen from 70% in 2010 to 53% in 2018.[[94]](#cite_note-95) The NHS is consistently ranked as the institution that makes people proudest to be British, beating the royal family, Armed Forces and the BBC.[[95]](#cite_note-96) One 2019 survey ranked nurses and doctors – not necessarily NHS staff – amongst the most trustworthy professions in the UK.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In November 2022 a survey by [Ipsos](/wiki/Ipsos "Ipsos") and the [Health Foundation](/wiki/Health_Foundation "Health Foundation") found just a third of respondents agreed the NHS gave a good service nationally, and 82% thought the NHS needed more funding. 62% expected care standards to fall during the following 12 months. Sorting out pressure and workload on staff and increasing staff numbers were the chief priorities the poll found. Improving A&E waiting times and routine services were also concerns.[[97]](#cite_note-98) Just 10% of UK respondents felt their government had the correct plans for the NHS. The Health Foundation stated in spite of these concerns, the public is committed to the founding principles of the NHS and 90% of respondents believe the NHS should be free, 89% believe NHS should provide a comprehensive service for everyone, and 84% believe the NHS should be funded mainly through taxation.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

### Role in combating COVID-19 pandemic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Role in combating COVID-19 pandemic")]

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom")

See also: [COVID-19 vaccination programme in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_programme_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 vaccination programme in the United Kingdom")

In 2020, the NHS issued medical advice in combating COVID-19 and partnered with tech companies to create computer dashboards to help combat the [nation's coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom").[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101) During the pandemic, the NHS also established integrated COVID into its 1-1-1 service line as well.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Following his discharge from the [St. Thomas' Hospital](/wiki/St._Thomas%27_Hospital "St. Thomas' Hospital") in London on 13 April 2020 after being diagnosed with [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19"), British Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") described NHS medical care as "astonishing" and said that the "NHS saved my life. No question".[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104) In this time, the NHS underwent major re-organisation to prepare for the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[104]](#cite_note-105)

On 5 July 2021, Queen Elizabeth II awarded the NHS the [George Cross](/wiki/George_Cross "George Cross").[[105]](#cite_note-:1-106) The George Cross, the highest award for gallantry available to civilians and is slightly lower in stature to the [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross"), is bestowed for acts of the greatest heroism or most conspicuous courage. In a handwritten note the Queen said the award was being made to all NHS staff past and present for their "courage, compassion and dedication" throughout the pandemic.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

In October 2022, as part of their efforts to tackle mental health challenges made worse by the pandemic, the NHS partnered with Israeli tech company Taliaz, stating that their AI-powered software translates complex genetic, demographic and clinical patient data into timesaving and cost-saving assessment, management and prescribing support tools for healthcare providers.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)

## Hospital beds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Hospital beds")]

In 2020, the UK had 2.43 hospital beds per 1,000 people.[[109]](#cite_note-110) In September 2017, the [King's Fund](/wiki/King%27s_Fund "King's Fund") documented the number of NHS hospital beds in England as 142,000, describing this as less than 50% of the number 30 years previously.[[110]](#cite_note-111) In 2019, one tenth of the beds in the UK were occupied by a patient who was alcohol-dependent.[[111]](#cite_note-112)

## Use of alternative medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Use of alternative medicine")]

Since 2000, the NHS has been under significant criticism for continuing to provide and trial the pseudoscientific methods of [alternative medicine](/wiki/Alternative_medicine "Alternative medicine"). A 2010 [Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") report noted that homeopathy "has been funded and provided on the NHS since its inception in 1948".[[112]](#cite_note-113)

The NHS also evaluated [the Lightning Process](/wiki/The_Lightning_Process "The Lightning Process") for paediatric CFS/ME in the SMILE trial - "The trial population was drawn from the Bath and Bristol NHS specialist paediatric CFS or ME service",[[113]](#cite_note-114) with the journal later acknowledging "the study was not fully ICMJE compliant".[[114]](#cite_note-115)

In clinical guidance, NICE's 2009 low-back-pain guideline has continued to tell clinicians to "Consider offering a course of [acupuncture](/wiki/Acupuncture "Acupuncture") needling comprising up to a maximum of 10 sessions over a period of up to 12 weeks".[[115]](#cite_note-116)

## Care mistakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Health_Service&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Care mistakes")]

A 2024 report by the public research university [Imperial College London](/wiki/Imperial_College_London "Imperial College London") said that the NHS spends £14.7 billion a year treating patients in England hurt by care mistakes.[[116]](#cite_note-117)
